name: Kinh Thánh Tấm Lòng Của Cha (Father's Heart Bible — Vietnamese)
script: Latin (chữ Quốc ngữ, with full diacritics)
register: Reverent contemporary Protestant Vietnamese, matching the Kinh Thánh Bản Truyền Thống Hiệu Đính 2011 (RVV11) — the revision of the beloved 1926 Bản Truyền Thống that Vietnamese evangelicals hold most dear. Crucially it adopts RVV11's intimate child-to-parent register 'chúng con' (the Lord's-Prayer 'Lạy Cha chúng con ở trên trời'), so the reader stands as a beloved child speaking to their own father, not a subject before a deity.
quote_convention: Double quotation marks “ ” for direct speech, nested single ‘ ’; some editions use guillemets « » — follow RVV11's straight-double style.
poetry_convention: Poetry (Psalms, prophets, canticles) set as verse: each poetic/parallel line on its own line, continuation lines indented one level, couplet parallelism preserved as in RVV11.

glossary.t: Cha Thiên Thượng của chúng con
glossary.note: Filial. 'chúng con' = the humble first-person children use toward a parent, so the reader is a child; 'Thiên Thượng' (heavenly/on-high) keeps the reverent height. Never Đức Chúa Trời.

Primary filial divine name. Exactly the RVV11 Lord's-Prayer form 'Cha chúng con'; the child-register 'con' (not neutral 'chúng tôi') is the warmth the vision requires. NEVER revert to Đức Chúa Trời / Đức Giê-hô-va.

Filial definite. Capitalized 'Cha' alone reads as THE Father; we avoid the creedal 'Đức Chúa Cha' (God-the-Lord-Father) because it foregrounds the deity-title Chúa over the child's own word for father.
Filial. Same 'Cha' stem with 'Thiên Thượng' for the on-high reverence; used in Vietnamese worship and hymnody.
Aramaic 'A-ba' retained per Rom 8:15 / Gal 4:6 tradition; the vocative particle 'ơi' is the tender cry a Vietnamese child gives to a parent, supplying Abba's intimacy that a bare 'Cha' would lose.
REVERSION / non-filial contrast term. Standard Protestant Vietnamese word for God-as-God. Contains no 'Cha' stem — use ONLY where English keeps 'God', never for 'our Father'.

REVERSION / non-filial contrast term. Bản Truyền Thống & RVV11 covenant name. Generic 'Lord' for Jesus = Chúa / Đức Chúa (distinct). Use ONLY where English keeps the covenant name; never for 'our Father'.
Standard Protestant Vietnamese (RVV11); not the Catholic 'Chúa Thánh Thần'. Confirmed register for John 3.

glossary.gloss: the Spirit of our Father (of us children)
glossary.note: Relational, not by-identity: uses 'Thần' as in Gen 1:2 ('Thần Đức Chúa Trời') but re-anchored to Cha, keeping the filial 'chúng con'. Contains the 'Cha' stem.

glossary.gloss: the Son of Man (Son of a human)
glossary.note: Fixed Christological idiom in every Vietnamese Bible; retained unchanged.
Capitalized 'Con' (the child's own word) as the relational counterpart to 'Cha'; avoids the creedal 'Đức Chúa Con' for the same filial reason as 'the Father'.

Traditional title where English legitimately keeps 'God' as God-as-God (RVV11). REVIEWER: confirm vs the filial alternative 'Con của Cha' (Son of the Father) — flagged parallel to Telugu తండ్రి కుమారుడు.
RVV11 John 3:16 wording ('Con Một'); warmer than the 1926 'Con độc sanh' (only-begotten). Carries the 'Con' stem.

glossary.gloss: the kingdom/realm of our Father (of us children)
glossary.note: Filial rendering of 'kingdom of God'. Builds on the beloved Lord's-Prayer phrase 'Nước Cha' (Your/the Father's kingdom); 'Nước' is warmer and more traditional than 'vương quốc'. Contains 'Cha'.
Protestant traditional spelling (as in 'Đức Chúa Jêsus'); not the Catholic 'Giêsu' nor BDM 'Giê-su'.
Traditional Protestant form; 'Đấng' is the honorific classifier for a holy person. Confirmed for John 3:28.
Transliteration with honorific 'Đấng'; semantic twin of 'Đấng Christ' (Anointed One), as in John 1:41.
Standard Vietnamese Bible term across all traditions.
Renders hesed as loyal covenant love; 'thành tín' (faithful, unwavering) carries the loyalty. REVIEWER: confirm vs the traditional 'sự nhân từ' (lovingkindness/mercy) used widely in Bản Truyền Thống.

glossary.gloss: compassion / tender mercy (of the inmost self)
glossary.note: Renders racham / splanchnizomai; 'lòng' (the seat of feeling, viscera) captures the gut-level tenderness, as in 'động lòng thương xót' = moved with compassion.
RVV11 form; updates the 1926 'sự công bình' (which reviewer may prefer for older readers).
The fixed John 3:16 wording in every Vietnamese Bible.

glossary.gloss: to be born again / born anew
glossary.note: RVV11 spelling 'sinh lại' (1926 'sanh lại'); John 3:3,7 also carries the sense 'born from above' (sinh lại từ trên cao).
